both_correct.caption: Histological features of nevi as seen in hematoxylin and eosin-stained slides, showing Type A melanocytes as large epithelioid cells at the top, and Type B melanocytes as small round blue cells at the bottom in the dermis.

Histological examination of a biopsy specimen taken from the polypoid nodule on the left cheek of an 81-year-old Japanese woman. The image shows a proliferation of atypical spindle cells and infiltrating lymphocytes on the dermis, features that contributed to the diagnosis of malignant melanoma (MM) at stage IIC. The section is stained with hematoxylin-and-eosin; original magnification, ×200.

Histopathology showing cutaneous melanoma cells with high heterogeneity in the cytomorphological parameters of the nucleoli. This panel displays cells containing micronucleoli (inconspicuous nucleoli) alongside cells with prominent macronucleoli. The image is shown at ×400 magnification, with an insert at ×600 magnification. Stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E).

High-power histologic view (Hematoxylin and eosin, ×100 original magnification) of the optic nerve head surface over the central vein. The image displays epithelioid pigmented melanoma cells, consistent with the tumor seeds of pigmented melanoma cells that lined the inner limiting membrane of the optic nerve and focally invaded the nerve head.

Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining showing histopathological features of a poorly differentiated invasive nodular melanoma, which measured 3.3 × 2.8 cm, with a Clark level of IV and a Breslow depth of 6 mm. The malignant neoplasm displays a solid growth pattern and forms nests (*).

Histopathology of a punch biopsy specimen from a blue-gray macule on the right buttock of a young adult. The patient presented with multiple, discrete blue-gray macules that had appeared a few years prior on the buttocks, lower back, and arms. This image, at x20 magnification, shows an ill-defined, deep dermal proliferation of pigmented dendritic melanocytes (highlighted by a red circle) with associated melanophages (highlighted by a black circle). No associated mitoses or necrosis were appreciated, consistent with common blue nevus. Stained with hematoxylin-eosin.

Microscopic image of histopathology for Case 31, a 79-year-old male with lentigo maligna melanoma (LMM) on the scalp. The image, stained with H&E and viewed at 100x magnification, shows evidence of lymphovascular invasion (LVI) on Level 4 of examination. In this specific case, the presence of LVI did not result in a change in the pT stage. The overall Breslow thickness for this case was 1.4 mm.

Histopathology from a plaque biopsy showing epidermal papillomatosis with acanthosis, associated with an increase in sebaceous glands and abortive hair follicles within the dermis, consistent with nevus sebaceous (H&E, 10× magnification).

Histopathology of gastric tissue from a 74-year-old male patient diagnosed with Primary Gastric Malignant Melanoma (PGMM). The tumor cells are round with hyperchromatic eccentric nuclei, a high nuclear/cytoplasmic ratio, rare nucleoli, and mixed eosinophilic and basophilic cytoplasm (hematoxylin and eosin staining, x40).
Histopathology showing a benign macule in the gingiva, corresponding to the macroscopic lesion in Panel A, stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (HE).

Histopathological examination of the surgically excised nodular mass from a female baby, diagnosed as neonatal congenital nodular melanoma. Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining shows the distribution of tumor cells in the dermis in sheets or nests. (Magnification × 100).

Histopathology of a conventional deep penetrating nevus. The melanocytes are epithelioid and spindled, possessing amphophilic cytoplasm. Admixed are scattered, often sparse to abundant, heavily pigmented melanophages. A lymphocytic infiltrate may also be present. There is no associated necrosis or loss or invasion of adnexal structures. (Hematoxylin and eosin, 100× magnification).

Histopathology of a resected gastric polyp from a 75-year-old man, diagnosed as malignant melanoma, stained with hematoxylin and eosin (HE) at 20×10 magnification, revealing melanoma cells with irregular morphology, melanin granules within the cytoplasm, and nuclear pleomorphism and atypia.

Microscopic examination of the initial shave biopsy from a 2 mm black papule on the left parietal scalp, revealing epidermotropic metastatic malignant melanoma. The image shows a follicle in the center of the specimen with a dense infiltrate of melanocytes in the dermis and extending into the overlying epidermis. Subsequent examination of the biopsy site showed pigment at the base. Hematoxylin and eosin stain; original magnification x = 4.

Histopathological appearance of malignant melanoma of the gallbladder, stained with hematoxylin and eosin (HE) at 200x magnification. Dense melanin pigments, appearing as abundant granular brown pigment, are found within the cytoplasm of the neoplastic cells.

Histopathology demonstrating malignant melanoma metastasis in the subcutaneous tissue of the abdomen. The image shows malignant melanocytes with nuclear atypia, characterized by atypical and hyperchromatic nuclei, and dense melanin pigmentation, presenting as abundant black pigment granules within the cytoplasm. The tumor cells are composed of elongated spindle cells and oval epithelioid cells arranged in sheets and bundles. Extracellular melanin pigment is also observed. Stained with hematoxylin and eosin, original magnification 200x.

Histopathology of the excised pigmented lesion from the cheek of a middle-aged woman, stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) and viewed on low-power (×10). This section reveals atypical intraepidermal melanocytic proliferation overlying dermal elastosis, consistent with a diagnosis of melanoma in situ on sun-damaged skin. The gold standard for LM/LMM diagnosis is skin biopsy, allowing visualization of dermatopathologic features like melanocyte atypia and non-uniform distribution on a background of extensive photodamage.
Histopathology of malignant melanoma, showing diffuse atypical cells infiltration forming a solid sheet (H&E, ×40).

A hematoxylin and eosin micrograph at 100x magnification from a biopsy confirming the diagnosis of Ota nevus. The image shows clusters of dendritic and fusiform melanocytes (indicated by black arrowheads) in the papillary dermis, diffusely infiltrating the collagen interstitium, around and near vessels (indicated by red asterisks). Melanophages (indicated by white arrowheads) are also observed in the dermis, extending into the reticular dermis, which collectively supported the pathological report of Ota nevus.

Histological features of nevi as seen in hematoxylin and eosin-stained slides, showing melanocytes at the basal layer (black arrows) that are spaced out, with intervening keratinocytes. A mitosis in a basal keratinocyte can also be seen (green arrow).

Histopathology showing an area of transition into heterologous osteoid differentiation within a melanoma, consistent with the presence of heterologous osteocartilaginous differentiation. The overall histopathology indicated melanoma with areas of heterologous osteocartilaginous differentiation, invading soft tissues and bone. The diagnosis of melanoma was confirmed by immunoreactivity with SOX10 and HMB45. Hematoxylin & eosin stain, original magnification ×400.

Histological image (hematoxylin-eosin, 10× magnification) of an NRAS- and CCND1 gene-mutated melanoma, 4.9 mm-thick with a mitotic rate of 11/mm², from the forearm of an 80-year-old woman. The image displays dilated capillaries (arrows) located beneath the epidermis and extending between tumor dermal aggregates. These dilated capillaries are a histological manifestation of the vascular patterns seen dermoscopically and are significantly associated with mutations in cell cycle genes like CCND1. This neoangiogenesis reflects the tumor's adaptive response to the increasing oxygen and nutrient demands of rapidly proliferating melanoma cells, often under hypoxic conditions, leading to the activation of factors like HIF-1α that induce the expression of genes involved in angiogenesis and melanoma progression.

This detailed histological view (hematoxylin and eosin stain, × 250 magnification) shows extensive tumour growth of anorectal melanoma beneath an intact surface squamous epithelium. This represents a typical histological morphology of primary anorectal melanoma, a condition where the tumour arises in the rectum, anal canal, or anal margin.

Histopathology showing a closeup of melanoma tumor cells at the anterior chamber angle, characterized by large epithelioid cells with clear cytoplasm and prominent nucleoli, alternating with spindle cells. This appearance is consistent with the histopathological examination of a diffuse iris ring-type melanoma involving the ciliary body, iris root, and anterior chamber angle structures, where epithelioid-type cells are seen at the tumor margins forming nests and invading the trabecular meshwork, and spindle B-type cells are also present. Stained with Hematoxylin and eosin, original magnification ×100.

Photomicrograph of a polypoidal mass of malignant melanoma, showing an endobronchial tumor with mucosal invasion. Histologically, the tumor is composed predominantly of epithelioid tumor cells, exhibiting junctional change and mitotic activity, with characteristic nesting of malignant cells beneath the bronchial epithelium, features suggestive of malignant melanoma. (Hematoxylin-Eosin ×50).

Histopathological examination showing solitary nodules, representing dermal separated nodes of a relapsed cell-rich blue nevus, located in close vicinity to the scar. The nodules are constituted by a nodular dermal proliferation of ovoid melanocytes with monomorphous nuclei and inconspicuous cytoplasm, intermingled with dendritic pigmented melanocytes. Nevus cells of the nodules are dispersed in the dermis and hyperpigmented, spindle-shaped melanocytes infiltrate among the collagen bundles. No features suggestive of malignancy, such as cytological atypia, atypical mitoses, or necrosis, are observed. Stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at 40× magnification. Inset shows a higher magnification view (H&E, 400×).

Histopathology showing malignant melanoma that had invaded the right lower lobe. The tumor cells exhibit large nuclei with high nuclear/cytoplasmic ratios, large and distinct nucleoli, and melanin particles in their cytoplasm. Stained with Hematoxylin and eosin (HE).

Histopathological examination of the excisional biopsy from a 76-year-old man's lower anterior alveolar gingiva, showing invasive tumor cells characteristic of nodular-type oral mucosal melanoma with heavy melanin pigmentation. This image highlights the typical characteristics of an oral mucosal melanoma (OMM). (Hematoxylin & eosin stain, ×200 magnification).

Histopathology from Patient 4 at 6 weeks old with a giant congenital melanocytic naevus, showing melanocytes present in storiform arrangements between desmoplastic collagen bundles. Stained with haematoxylin and eosin, original magnification × 100.

Histopathology of Nodular Melanoma (NM) showing ulceration of the epidermis overlying the tumor, characterized by fibrin deposition and neutrophils. Tumor ulceration was a significant finding, present in 47 (59.5%) of the melanomas studied. (Hematoxylin and Eosin stain, original magnification × 100).

Histopathology showing invasive subungual melanoma (SUM), where the basal and suprabasal layers of the nail matrix and bed epithelium and dermis are invaded by an excessive number of melanocytes often displaying severe cellular atypia. Stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E), original magnification ×4.

Histopathology of the black nodule showing an intradermal proliferation of pigmented dendritic melanocytes within a fibrotic stroma. The image reveals no features suggestive of malignancy, such as cytological or nuclear atypia, or mitotic activity. These findings are consistent with the diagnosis of common Blue Nevus (BN). (Hematoxylin-eosin stain; original magnification ×20).

Histopathology (hematoxylin-eosin stain, 10× magnification) showing superficial spreading melanoma infiltrating the superficial dermis, with a Breslow thickness of 0.7 mm. The melanoma exhibits moderate non-brisk peritumoral and intratumoral inflammatory infiltrate. Histologically, superficial spreading melanoma is characterized by asymmetric, poorly demarcated melanocytes that lack cellular maturation. Melanocytes are distributed unevenly, often with solitary melanocytes being more predominant over nests, and commonly with evidence of intraepithelial spread in a pagetoid manner.

Histopathology at ×20 magnification, illustrating amelanotic variants of melanoma cells. These cells lack melanin, making them challenging to diagnose in routine hematoxylin and eosin (H&E)-stained specimens and potentially leading to misidentification with non-melanocytic tumors. The scale bar represents 50 μm.

Histopathological evaluation (hematoxylin-eosin, x200) showing bone tissue of the orbital floor with focal infiltration of melanoma cells, despite overall negative surgical margins reported for bone orbital walls.

Histopathological imaging of superficial spreading melanoma, showing an asymmetrical proliferation of atypical epithelioid melanocytes with abundant cytoplasm, fine melanin pigmentation, and pleomorphic vesicular nuclei with prominent eosinophilic nucleoli, extending across all layers of the epidermis. H&E stain (X200).

Histopathological examination showing a proliferative growth centered in the upper dermis with marked thinning of the epidermis. These features were observed during histopathological examination (HPE) that led to the diagnosis of malignant melanoma. (Hematoxylin and eosin stain, ×40 magnification).

Histopathology of the resected lesion showing multiple anaplastic cells and mitotic figures, stained with Hematoxylin and eosin (x400). This high magnification confirmed melanoma and showed extensive anaplasia and mitotic activity.

High-magnification histopathology (H&E, ×200 magnification) from a 28-year-old man diagnosed with superficial spreading invasive melanoma. This view shows atypical melanocytes present in irregular nests and as single units extending throughout all layers of the epidermis.

Higher magnification histopathology of the amelanotic melanoma tumor (hematoxylin-eosin stain, original magnification ×400). This view highlights prominent nuclear mitosis and atypia within the intermediate-grade tumor cells, further supporting the diagnosis of amelanotic melanoma.

Histopathology of Superficial Spreading Melanoma (SSM) showing brisk tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs). Brisk TILs were present in 26 cases (32.9%) of the melanomas studied. (Hematoxylin and Eosin stain, original magnification × 100).
Pathological examination showing histopathology of malignant melanoma, which was revealed with a Breslow thickness of 1.6 mm, stained with H&E (×300).

Histopathology of malignant melanoma (H&E stain, 100× magnification). The image shows atypical melanocytes with nuclear pleomorphism and prominent nucleoli. Tumor cells are arranged in nests with scattered brown melanin pigment, typical of malignant melanoma.

Histopathology image showing nodular melanoma (NM) with prominent nuclear inclusions in atypical epithelioid cells. The primary tumor biopsy for this case also presented strong immune positivity for SOX10, HMB-45, and Melan-A. (hematoxylin–eosin staining, magnification 380-fold).
Photomicrograph of a Spitz nevus histological section stained with hematoxylin and eosin (HE) for light optical microscopy, showing epithelial expansion with free borders (40x magnification).

Microscopic picture of the surgical specimen stained with H&E at ×40 magnification. Histological examination shows the presence of fibro-connective and lymphatic tissue containing malignant neoplastic cells, compatible with a nodule of melanoma inside the buccal fat pad. The image shows proliferating atypical cells containing coarse-brownish granules suspected to be melanin.

Histopathology of a Nevus Cell Nevus (NCN) from patient 3 (slide number 1256), stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E). This NCN sample serves as a representative image for the immunohistochemical staining results shown in subsequent panels. Scale bar represents 200 μm. Overview inserts are two times lower in magnification.

Histopathology from a shave biopsy demonstrating melanocytes with severe cytologic atypia, characterized by large pleomorphic and hyperchromatic nuclei with irregular contours, prominent nucleoli, and numerous mitotic figures (black arrowheads). This dermally based proliferation of large nodules is consistent with melanoma, which showed a mitotic rate of 25 figures per mm² among other prognostic factors. (H&E, 200× magnification).

Histopathological features of the back nodule showing the conventional component of the malignant melanoma. The tumor is composed of large polygonal cells with large round nuclei. The majority of these tumor cells contain melanin pigment. The image is stained with hematoxylin and eosin (magnification, ×200).

Histopathology of nodular melanoma, showing a solid growth pattern with epidermal ulceration and deep invasion into the reticular dermis. The overall tumor had a Breslow depth of invasion of 3.51 mm, leading to a diagnosis of pT3b nodular melanoma. (H&E, 40× magnification).

Histopathology image showing features of nevus sebaceous (NS) in the background. The image depicts epidermal hyperplasia with sebaceous glands opening directly to the surface. The background skin from which this tissue was taken also exhibited hyperkeratosis, epidermal papillomatosis, and acanthosis, along with apocrine glands in the deep dermis, all consistent with a nevus sebaceous background. The tissue sections were formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded, and stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). The image was captured at ×40 magnification.

Histopathology of the excised pigmented lesion from the cheek of a middle-aged woman, stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) and viewed on higher-power (×20). This magnification provides a clearer view of the atypical intraepidermal melanocytic proliferation, which was found to extend to all surgical margins, leading to a diagnosis of melanoma in situ on sun-damaged skin. Key dermatopathologic features consistent with this diagnosis include melanocyte atypia and their non-uniform distribution within the epidermis, often seen with extensive photodamage.

Histopathology of the initial excision from a 78-year-old man's enlarging erythematous mass on his left posterior shoulder, showing a large nodule within the dermis, consistent with melanoma (confirmed by immunohistochemical analysis). This panel highlights pseudopapillary architecture within the nodule, lined by malignant amelanotic plasmacytoid cells. The cells exhibit abundant cytoplasm, eccentric nuclei, and pale paranuclear zones. Stained with Hematoxylin & Eosin (H&E) at 100× magnification.

Histopathology showing a subepithelial cystic nevus (hematoxylin and eosin, original magnification ×200). In this study, subepithelial nevi comprised 24% (n=25) of the excised lesions, and 36% (9/25) of these subepithelial nevi were observed to be cystic. Overall, 60% of all nevi in the study were cystic.

Histopathology of a liver section from patient 1, showing micrometastases of uveal melanoma. This panel shows a higher magnification (×40) view of the framed area indicated in Panel A, illustrating the predominantly infiltrative or replacement growth pattern of the micrometastases. This image provides an example of micrometastases of uveal melanoma, a condition detected in 10 out of 11 (91%) patients in the study. Stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). Magnification, ×40.

Histopathology demonstrating malignant melanoma metastasis in the subcutaneous tissue of the abdomen. The image shows malignant melanocytes with nuclear atypia, characterized by atypical and hyperchromatic nuclei, and dense melanin pigmentation, presenting as abundant black pigment granules within the cytoplasm. The tumor cells are composed of elongated spindle cells and oval epithelioid cells arranged in sheets and bundles. Extracellular melanin pigment is also observed. Stained with hematoxylin and eosin, original magnification 100x.

Histopathology of the penile specimen (H&E stain, 200x magnification) from a 62-year-old male patient diagnosed with mucosal melanoma of the glans penis. Below the non-keratinizing squamous epithelium of the glans, an in situ component and pagetoid spread are observed, specifically found in the epithelium of the external urethral orifice. Lymphovascular invasion was also noted at the tumor’s edge. The tumor cells display diffuse PRAME expression (PRAME immunohistochemistry, 100x magnification), which is another marker for melanoma.

Histopathology (40× hematoxylin & eosin [H&E] stain) showing the central area of the lesion, which is mainly an intradermal combination of type C and dendritic (blue nevus-like) melanocytes. The final histopathological diagnosis was acral compound melanocytic nevus with congenital features.

Histopathology (Hematoxylin and Eosin, HE, ×100) of melanoma in situ on the left flank of a 62-year-old man. The image shows a proliferation of atypical melanocytes, present in single units and nests, extending through all layers of the epidermis. Additionally, a dense lymphohistiocytic infiltrate with melanophages is visible in the dermis.

Histological detail (H&E; original magnification 200×) from the enucleated eye of a 60-year-old female patient with choroidal melanoma. The image illustrates the abrupt transition between the vital tumor (at the bottom) and radiation-induced necrosis (at the top). Radiation-induced necrosis is characterized by a sharp and well-defined demarcation from the residual viable tumor tissue and a high degree of pigmentation, often more pronounced than the surrounding viable tumor, due to copious melanophage infiltration. This patient underwent secondary enucleation 29 months after proton beam radiotherapy due to tumor regrowth.

Postoperative histopathological examination of melanoma from a 28-year-old male patient with right cerebellopontine angle melanoma. Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining at ×400 magnification shows heteromorphous large cells with obvious nucleoli.

Histopathology of the deep dermis showing a tumor with a predominant spindle cell type, characteristic of atypical melanocytes. The tumor cells are arranged in an irregular branching pattern with intervening fibrous septae. These spindle cells appear fusiform to round in different orientations. This presentation, with spindle cell predominance and collagenous stroma, is suggestive of desmoplastic malignant melanoma. Histopathological examination confirmed the diagnosis of malignant melanoma. Atypical features such as mitosis, multi-nucleation, pleomorphism, hyperchromatism, increased nuclear cytoplasmic ratio, and prominent nucleoli are seen within the tumor. The image is stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) and viewed at ×10 magnification.

Close-up histopathology of a melanoma metastasis located in an inguinal lymph node, showing both conventional melanoma and vascular components. The image is stained with hematoxylin and eosin at ×20 magnification.

Low magnification histopathology showing an ovoidal-shaped, poorly-pigmented choroidal tumor with sharply circumscribed margins. This tumor is located in the posterior ocular chamber, protruding into the posterior segment of the eye, and is consistent with a 'balloon cell melanoma of the choroid'. The surgical sample was fixed in neutral-buffered 10% formalin and submitted for histologic examination. Staining is hematoxylin and eosin (original magnification 25×).
Histopathology of an excision specimen showing malignant melanocytes within the lymph node. The image is taken at 200x magnification and stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E).

Microscopic findings of the labia minora lesion, stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) at 40× magnification, confirming a diagnosis of Nevus Sebaceus (NS). The image shows plenty of enlarged sebaceous glands, slight hyperkeratosis, and an acanthotic epidermis with papillomatosis. The surface of the lesion is partially covered with proliferative squamous epithelium and displays papillary hyperplasia. A large number of proliferative, well-differentiated sebaceous glands lacking hair follicles are found under the skin and deep dermis.

Histopathology of an esophageal tumor biopsy showing a proliferation of epithelioid and spindle-shaped cells arranged in fascicles, with the presence of melanin pigment. This tumor, located in the junction medium-distal esophagus, was identified as a 2 cm, lobulated, and pigmented mass, leading to a diagnosis of primary esophageal melanoma. Hematoxylin and eosin (HE) stain, original magnification ×40.

Histopathological examination with hematoxylin and eosin staining at high-power magnification (40×) confirming the diagnosis of Verrucous Epidermal Nevus (VEN). The image exhibits the stratum corneum with prominent lamellar hyperkeratosis (black star).

Photomicrograph showing a lightly pigmented conjunctival melanoma at higher magnification (×200). The tumor (indicated by a black arrow) is composed of atypical melanocytes. Some of these neoplastic cells contain fine granular brown pigment, identified as melanin, within their cytoplasm. The image is stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (HE).

Histopathologic findings of the mass excised from the left ear canal of a 33-year-old woman, showing numerous nests and groups of melanocytic nevus cells located in the dermis, covered with normal skin. These findings were compatible with an intradermal nevus, but the nevus contained atypical melanocytes without pleomorphism, leading to a final diagnosis of a dysplastic nevus within an intradermal nevus (Hematoxylin-eosin, original magnification ×10).

Histopathology from the spleen of a 72-year-old male patient, showing replacement of the splenic parenchyma by a malignant neoplasm, consistent with metastasis from cutaneous malignant melanoma. Residual lymphoid cells are observed at the periphery (black arrow). The patient presented with elevated lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) levels and a solitary 4 × 3 × 3 cm solid lesion in the spleen, making this a rare case of isolated splenic metastasis 25 years after the initial diagnosis. Hematoxylin and eosin-stained section, magnification × 10.

Histopathology showing spindle-shaped nevoid cells with scattered intracytoplasmic melanin pigment granules, indicated by an arrow. The section is stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) at an original magnification of ×400.

Histopathology of malignant melanoma, specifically the predominant spindle cell type consistent with desmoplastic malignant melanoma, showing atypical melanocytes. These tumor cells exhibit prominent atypical features including mitosis, multi-nucleation, pleomorphism, hyperchromatism, increased nuclear cytoplasmic ratio, and prominent nucleoli. The cells have a clear cytoplasm and hyperchromatic nuclei, proliferating from the dermal-epidermal junction into the connective tissue, often appearing fusiform to round with an irregular branching pattern and intervening fibrous septae. Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) stain, ×40 magnification.

Histopathology showing melanoma cells characterized by atypia, pleomorphism, evident nucleoli, and frequent mitotic figures. This image depicts the melanoma component of a tumor that also showed areas of heterologous osteocartilaginous differentiation, invading soft tissues and bone, and was confirmed by immunoreactivity with SOX10 and HMB45. (Hematoxylin & eosin, ×400).
Histopathology of a right inguinal lymph node biopsy stained with hematoxylin and eosin, showing evidence of melanoma at 100x magnification.

Histopathology from an excisional biopsy of a 0.9cm x 0.9cm indurated nodule on the right posteromedial calf, revealing a malignant proliferation of partially cohesive epithelioid cells with large nucleoli and frequent mitoses. These features are characteristic of the malignant melanoma component of a collision tumor, which also tested positive for Mart-1, HMB45, and SOX-10. Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) stain, original magnification 40x.

Histopathology (H&E, x40 magnification) of oral mucosal melanoma (OMM) showing a poorly circumscribed pigmented tumor infiltrating the underlying tissue, with overlying mucosa. The tumor is composed of oval to spindle cells arranged in diffuse sheets and nests, exhibiting moderate nuclear pleomorphism, vesicular to hyperchromatic nuclei, prominent eosinophilic nucleoli, and both intracellular and extracellular pigment, consistent with the diagnosis of OMM.

Histopathologic examination of Case 3, a superficial spreading type melanoma with a MAP2K1 in-frame deletion, illustrates the radial growth phase. It is characterized by a confluent proliferation of atypical melanocytes along the basal epidermis, showing epidermal involvement and extension beyond the dermal component. This image also demonstrates confluent growth along the basal epidermis with rete effacement. Stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at ×200 magnification.

Histopathology image of a superficial spreading melanoma (T1a, Breslow 0.5 mm) on the abdomen of an 82-year-old man, stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (HE) at 100× original magnification. This higher magnification image shows atypical melanocytes in single units and irregular nests mostly arranged at the dermo-epidermal junction (DEJ), along with melanophages in the dermis. These features correlate with the structureless bichromatic brown-blue areas (labeled +) seen in dermoscopy. Superficial spreading melanoma is a biologically slow-growing melanoma, typically showing an initial radial growth phase involving the epidermis and DEJ, followed by a vertical growth phase (VGP) with invasion into the dermis.

Histopathology from the cutaneous malignant melanoma of the scalp (primary tumor, 25 years prior) in a 72-year-old male patient, showing malignant cells with pagetoid spread into the epidermis (red arrow). The tumor cells are arranged in a nesting pattern in the dermis, displaying round nuclei and vacuolated and pink cytoplasm. A dermal host response with lymphocytic infiltrate is also visible. This case is notable for the later development of an isolated splenic metastasis 25 years after the initial diagnosis of cutaneous malignant melanoma. Hematoxylin and eosin-stained section, magnification × 20.

Histopathology of the lesion from an excisional biopsy, diagnosed as a collision tumor involving a trichofolliculoma (TF) and melanocytic nevus. This H&E stained image at ×2 magnification provides a full view of the lesion, revealing a central cystic cavity containing detritus and hair fragments (keratinous debris and hair shaft fragments), with numerous hair follicles arising from the cyst wall.

Histopathological examination of a hyperpigmented lesion showing an H&E-stained section at 100x magnification. The image reveals an atypical melanocytic lesion composed of aggregates of pigmented cells (denoted by arrows) in the superficial lamina propria, located between or around benign pits. These aggregates consist of atypical melanocytes exhibiting nuclear atypia. The overall findings from such lesions were confirmatory of malignant melanoma, which also harbored a BRAF V60E mutation identified by targeted next-generation sequencing.

Histopathology of a biopsy specimen from a skin lesion on a 3-day-old female infant, consistent with nevus sebaceus. The image shows hyperkeratosis, acanthosis, numerous sebaceous glands, and malformed hair follicles. There are also proliferative changes of sebaceous glands and apocrine glands within the epidermis. The section is stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) and viewed at 100x magnification.

Histopathology, stained with hematoxylin, phloxin, and safranin, showing an intermediate view of a cellular area within a melanoma that originated as a relapse from a BAP1-inactivated nevus. This transformation is exceptional and occurred during childhood, with the initial lesion identified at age 11. The image shows admixed small and large cells, characteristic of the dense, clonal sheet-like areas that support the diagnosis of melanoma associated with germline BAP1 mutations.

Histopathology showing the lymphatic nevus cell embolus demonstrating characteristic morphological features of type A nevus cells. These features include a round to cuboidal shape, well-defined cell borders, abundant cytoplasm, and nest formation. Additionally, the embolus was lined by flattened endothelial cells, which are identical to the endothelial cells lining the lymphatic lumen. Stained with hematoxylin and eosin; original magnification, ×400.
Histopathological evaluation of a biopsy performed on an anal lesion, demonstrating malignant melanoma. The image is stained with hematoxylin and eosin at ×4 magnification.

Histopathology of the initial excision from a 78-year-old man's enlarging erythematous mass on his left posterior shoulder, showing a large nodule within the dermis, consistent with melanoma (confirmed by immunohistochemical analysis). This panel highlights pseudopapillary architecture within the nodule, lined by malignant amelanotic plasmacytoid cells. The cells exhibit abundant cytoplasm, eccentric nuclei, and pale paranuclear zones. Stained with Hematoxylin & Eosin (H&E) at 100× magnification.

Histopathology of the nail unit, stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) at ×100 magnification, demonstrating atypical melanocytes characterized by hyperchromatic nuclei, which have invaded the dermis layer.

Histopathology of malignant melanoma metastasis, depicting neoplastic melanoma cells infiltrating the lamina propria. The neoplastic cells are small and show minimal nuclear pleomorphism, consistent with nuclear pleomorphism being detected in all five cases. This represents a diffuse/interstitial growth pattern, observed in four of the five studied melanoma cases, which also appeared amelanotic. Original magnification, x100; Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) stain.

Photomicrograph showing histopathological features of an excised orbital mass, consistent with a mixed spindle cell and epitheloid cell melanoma. The image displays a sheet of plump epitheloid tumor cells with large nuclei and prominent nucleoli. Melanin is absent in the tumor cells, consistent with the largely amelanotic nature of this melanoma. (H&E, ×400 magnification)

Photomicrograph showing a Spitz nevus harboring an HRAS mutation, observed at 200× magnification with H&E staining. The image displays a symmetric, exophytic, and predominantly intradermal melanocytic proliferation with a flat base. It is composed of fascicles of large spindle melanocytes characterized by amphophilic cytoplasm, moderately pleomorphic vesicular nuclei, and distinct nucleoli. These melanocytes are intermingled with thick collagen bundles, contributing to a desmoplastic appearance, a common feature in Spitz neoplasms with HRAS aberrations. These lesions tend to be symmetric with an infiltrative base, and while mitoses are usually rare and not atypical, moderate cytological atypia can be present in a significant percentage of HRAS-mutated cases.

Histopathology showing a benign Spitz nevus from the back of a 26-year-old female. The lesion was submitted as “indeterminate; favor atypical Spitz tumor,” but was subsequently classified as a benign Spitz nevus by all 3 reviewing dermatopathologists. The lesion received a score of –7.2. Hematoxylin-eosin stain; original magnification x100.

Histopathological section from the excised parotid gland lesion, stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at 20× magnification. The image shows spindle-shaped malignant cells, consistent with spindle cell melanoma. Microscopic analysis further revealed nuclear pleomorphism and prominent nucleoli, characteristic features of the malignant neoplasm.

Histopathological image showing hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining of tumor cells from Case 3, magnified 400x. These tumor cells tested positive for estrogen receptor (ER) and progesterone receptor (PR), with expression observed in their nuclei. This melanoma developed and spread rapidly after childbirth.

Histopathology illustrating the various morphologic appearances of neoplastic cells in anorectal melanoma. Panel D shows spindle cells, characterized by elongated nuclei and sparse cytoplasm. Spindle cell types, either alone or in combination with other cell types, were a frequent finding in the systematically reviewed cases of anorectal melanoma in this study. The tumors frequently presented as polypoid (73%) and most (67%) demonstrated pigmentation. The mean tumor thickness across cases was 10.4 mm, and ulceration was observed in 80% of cases. (Hematoxylin & Eosin, x400 magnification).

Histopathology illustrating the base continuity with a steep indentation between two distinct melanomas, composed of pigmented epithelioid melanoma cells, from the enucleated right eyeball. The section is stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (HE) at ×200 magnification.

Histopathology showing a basal cell naevus (BCN) in a haematoxylin and eosin (H&E) stained tissue section. The BCN consists of tiny arborescent buddings of cytologically bland basaloid cells, which are either attached to the epidermis or replacing the hair follicles. This lesion was identified from one of the multiple skin-coloured papules found on the patient's face and neck, with its diagnosis confirmed by H&E staining of the excised tissue.

Histopathology of an adjacent macule/papule presenting into the plaque and included into the excision, showing features of a common blue nevus. Stained with hematoxylin–eosin, original magnification ×40.

Histopathology (H&E, ×400 magnification) of a punch biopsy taken from a follicular lesion, consistent with Becker's nevus. The image shows skin with epidermis exhibiting focal mild hyperpigmentation of basal keratinocytes, a darkly pigmented basal cell layer, mild acanthosis, and regular elongation of rete ridges. The superficial dermis shows minimal perivascular lymphoplasmacytic infiltrates.

Histopathology of gastric tissue from a 74-year-old male patient diagnosed with Primary Gastric Malignant Melanoma (PGMM). The image shows infiltration of the gastric mucosa by a tumor consisting of large atypical epithelioid or spindle cells growing in solid sheets and fascicles (hematoxylin and eosin staining, x20).

Photomicrograph of balloon cell melanoma from the first case (female, 76 years old), showing the pseudolipoblastic/balloon cell aspects of the melanocytes, characterized by a swollen histological appearance and the disintegration of disordered and abundant melanic pigment. The lesion consists of more than 50% “balloon-shaped” melanocytes, featuring abundant and finely vacuolized cytoplasm and hyperchromatic nuclei generally located in the periphery of the cell, but not pycnotic. Melanin is quantitatively reduced within the cell, with a disordered dispersion within the lesion itself and in numerous melanophages. Architecturally, the cells are organized in large pale masses that replace the dermis and thin the epidermis, forming large solid sheets of “ballooniform” melanocytes divided into irregular aggregates by thin collagenous septa, with no clear signs of activity at the dermo-epidermal junction or pagetoid spreading. Very rare mitoses were observed. Hematoxylin-Eosin stain, Original Magnification 4×.

Histopathology (Hematoxylin Eosin, original magnification 70x) showing Non-familial collagenoma, a type of Connective Tissue Nevus (CTN). This panel demonstrates thickened collagen bundles arranged randomly in the reticular dermis (long arrow). The presence of grossly thickened collagen fibers is a most important histological feature that allowed confirmation of CTN diagnosis in all patients, many of whom were initially suspected of having Juvenile Localized Scleroderma (JLS).

Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining, at higher magnification, showing histopathological features of a poorly differentiated invasive nodular melanoma. Pleomorphic, polygonal cells with vesicular nuclei, prominent nucleoli, and abundant cytoplasm are observed. Note the pigment in the cytoplasm of the cells, which corresponds to melanin.

Histopathologic examination of the deep aspect of the tumorigenic component in a melanoma with a MAP2K1 in-frame deletion reveals nodules of atypical epithelioid cells. The dermal components universally consisted of variably sized nodules and nests, with this image showcasing the epithelioid cytomorphology noted in the majority of cases. Melanocytes typically have medium to large nuclei with heterogenous chromatin, variably prominent nucleoli, and moderate to severe nuclear pleomorphism. Stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at ×200 magnification.

Histopathological appearance of malignant melanoma of the gallbladder, stained with hematoxylin and eosin (HE) at 200x magnification. Junctional activity is present, characterized by aggregates of malignant melanoma cells identified at the junction of the epithelium and lamina propria. The tumor was also observed to focally invade the muscularis layer.

Histopathology of the penile specimen (H&E stain, 400x magnification) from a 62-year-old male patient diagnosed with mucosal melanoma of the glans penis. Brisk mitotic activity with atypical cell divisions is evident, indicated by the yellow arrowhead. Histopathological examination revealed severe cytological atypia and numerous mitotic figures. The inset presents Ki-67-positive cells (Ki-67 immunohistochemistry, 200x magnification) with a proliferation index estimated at 80% positivity in the hot-spot areas, indicating rapid tumor growth.

Histopathology of nodular melanoma, stained with hematoxylin-eosin at 10× magnification. The image displays a densely packed dermal proliferation of atypical melanocytes extending to the reticular dermis, consistent with Clark’s level IV and a Breslow thickness of 2.9 mm. Several dermal mitoses are evident, counted at 10 mitoses per 1 mm². Nodular melanoma lesions frequently exhibit such dermal mitosis and may also present with epidermal ulceration. Histologically, a key diagnostic feature for nodular melanoma is that if an intraepidermal component is present, it should not extend for more than three rete ridges beyond the dermal component.

Histopathology displaying epidermal papillomatosis (indicated by black arrow) with numerous large sebaceous glands (indicated by red arrow) directly attached to the overlying acanthotic epidermis (indicated by blue arrow), consistent with a nevus sebaceus. Additionally, a neurofibroma is observed (indicated by green arrow). This image was obtained from a lesion whose histopathology, following complete elliptical excision, showed the characteristic features of epidermal papillomatosis with numerous large sebaceous glands directly attached to the overlying acanthotic epidermis. Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining, 20x magnification.
Histological examination showing proliferation of nevus cells and melanocytes visible on the tip of the rete ridges. Stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at ×40 magnification.

Histopathology showing dermal melanoma, epithelioid subtype, from a 77-year-old male patient who presented with a giant oozing, necrotic tumor on the scalp. Histological analysis of a biopsy sample (collected before the tumor reached nodular shape) revealed BRAF-mutant melanoma with isolated neoplastic infiltration under the epidermis, lacking an epidermal component and evidence of regression. Breslow thickness and Clark level could not be assessed. The tumor exhibits a high proliferation index of Ki-67 80% and non-brisk tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs). Epithelioid polygonal cells show mitotic activity of 15/1 mm², and the neoplastic mass has focal necrosis. (hematoxylin–eosin staining, magnification 50-fold).

Histopathology showing cerebroid nests of melanocytes (CNMs) located at the periphery or growth edge of a nodular melanoma. Cerebroid nests of melanocytes were observed in the periphery of 60% of melanomas in this study, with a higher number commonly found in the peripheral zone of the tumor. The image is a hematoxylin and eosin stained slide. Magnification 4x, scale bars 200 µm.

Histopathology of a biopsy taken from a lesion on the patient's right femoral area, which led to a diagnosis of post-CT eruptive compound nevus. The image displays nest structures composed of nevus cells with oval nuclei and eosinophilic cytoplasm, originating from the dermoepidermal junction and extending toward the lower dermis. Hematoxylin and eosin stain, original magnification ×40.

both_wrong.caption: Higher magnification histopathology (hematoxylin-eosin stain, original magnification x 200) of the melanocytic nevus with partial BRCA1-associated protein-1 (BAP1)-inactivation (BAPoma) from a 23-year-old female patient. The image reveals nests and strands of relatively monomorphic epithelioid cells with isolated mitoses (top right). This specific histology is compatible with partial BAP1 inactivation.

Histopathologic appearance of acral lentiginous melanoma in situ, showing increased single unit atypical melanocytes with 3 mitoses in a high-power field. Stained with hematoxylin-eosin at 400× original magnification.

Acral melanoma. Histopathology at higher magnification, showing the characteristics of tumor cells in acral lentiginous melanoma (ALM). The atypical melanocytes are hyperchromatic, consistent with enlarged hyperchromatic nuclei, and exhibit a cytoplasmic fixation retraction artefact (arrowhead). These cells can be spindle or epithelioid. The dermis contains a chronic inflammatory cell infiltrate, which is often described as band-like and surrounding the tumors, associated with macrophages (arrow). (H&E, ×400).

Histopathology of a lesion from the back of a 58-year-old male. This lesion was initially submitted with a pretest diagnosis of “benign; favor severely dysplastic nevus,” but all three reviewing dermatopathologists classified it as melanoma. After the test, the submitting dermatopathologist changed the diagnosis to melanoma in situ. The image is stained with Hematoxylin-eosin; original magnification x200.

Histopathology of skin showing malignant melanoma (Clark's level V, Breslow thickness 45 mm) with infiltrating melanin pigment containing epithelioid and spindle tumor cells. Features include brisk mitoses (3 to 5 mitoses/high-power field) and junctional activity. (Hematoxylin and Eosin stain, ×100 magnification).

Histopathology of a large Cellular blue nevus (CBN) demonstrating a dumbbell-shaped appearance (original magnification ×20). CBN typically lacks overt atypia and high mitotic activity (usually <1/mm²). This image is stained with hematoxylin-eosin.

Histopathology showing melanocytic nevi (NN) in a sentinel lymph node, observed in H&E staining at ×100 magnification. Small groups of melanocytic cells are visible within the nodal capsule (indicated by an arrow), exhibiting bland cytomorphology. NN are growth-arrested melanocytic clusters typically diagnosed in sentinel lymph nodes draining melanoma, breast, vulvar, or penile cancer. Unlike most melanomas, NN usually have bland cytomorphology and no mitotic activity. The localization of these melanocytes within the capsule or trabeculae of the lymph node strongly supports the diagnosis of a benign NN.

Histologic examination (hematoxylin and eosin, magnification, ×10) revealed proliferation of heavily pigmented melanocytes, mature nevus cells, and abundant melanin-laden macrophages in the underlying stroma, with multiple cysts, consistent with a pigmented cystic compound nevus of the conjunctiva. No evidence of pagetoid spread or dysplasia was detected.

Histopathology of vulvar melanoma in situ. Photomicrograph of the excised specimen showing confluent, severely atypical melanocytes with pagetoid spread. This characteristic proliferation of enlarged, poorly nested and confluent, severely atypical melanocytes was revealed by the histopathological examination of the excised tissue, confirming melanoma in situ. (Hematoxylin-eosin stain, original magnification ×400).

Histopathology showing melanocytic nevi (NN) in a sentinel lymph node, observed in H&E staining at ×200 magnification. Small groups of melanocytic cells are visible within the nodal capsule (indicated by an arrow), exhibiting bland cytomorphology. NN are growth-arrested melanocytic clusters typically diagnosed in sentinel lymph nodes draining melanoma, breast, vulvar, or penile cancer. Unlike most melanomas, NN usually have bland cytomorphology and no mitotic activity. The localization of these melanocytes within the capsule or trabeculae of the lymph node strongly supports the diagnosis of a benign NN.

Microscopic image of vulvar malignant melanoma, from a 58-year-old female presenting with a gray-white irregular mass on the right labia minora. Histologic examination of the vulvar tissue revealed a large number of tumor cells containing dark-brown intracellular pigment. The patient's tumor synchronously involved the vulva and uterine cervix. Stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E), original magnification ×100.
Histopathology of melanoma as seen in a hematoxylin and eosin-stained slide, demonstrating abnormal maturation. Large nests of melanocytes can be seen from top to bottom of the lesion.

Histopathology (hematoxylin and eosin, H&E stain) showing a junctional amelanotic proliferation with a lentiginous pattern of growth, intraepidermal ascending cells, and deep follicular extension. A punch biopsy of the tumor revealed a predominantly lentiginous proliferation within the basal layer of the epidermis, with forming nests, consisting of epithelioid atypical cells, including areas of thinning of the epidermis with loss of rete ridges, and indefinite borders. Severe solar elastosis was present within the dermis, and no melanin pigmentation was identified. The diagnosis was amelanotic lentigo maligna melanoma with a Breslow thickness of 0.7 mm.

Histopathologic findings of a dark brown, dome-shaped mass excised from the posterior wall of the left ear canal's cartilaginous portion in a 33-year-old woman. The image shows scattered clusters composed of round to oval-shaped melanocytic cells (black arrow) located in the dermis, consistent with a dysplastic nevus within an intradermal nevus, due to the presence of atypical melanocytes without pleomorphism. The image is stained with Hematoxylin-eosin, original magnification ×200.

Histopathologic findings of malignant melanoma after repeated recurrent dysplastic nevi. The tumor cells show round to oval nuclei with mild nuclear pleomorphism, small nucleoli, and an irregular nuclear membrane. The cytoplasm contains melanin pigmentation. No mitosis is evident (Hematoxylin and Eosin, ×400 magnification).
Histopathology showing sharp demarcation in a focal atypical cell nodule of a congenital melanocytic nevus. Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) stain, original magnification ×40.

Histopathology of a Spitz tumor demonstrating marked tumor asymmetry, which is identified as a risk-associated histopathological feature and a proposed prognostic parameter indicating increased clinical risk for patients. Stained with hematoxylin and eosin, original magnification ×25.

Histopathology of a superficial spreading melanoma from a 21-year-old patient. The melanocytic lesion is characterized by atypical junctional activity and extensive regression in the superficial dermis. This image depicts the regression at the margins of the lesion. The full histological evaluation confirmed a diagnosis of superficial spreading melanoma, Breslow 0.70 mm, Clark level three, pT1a. Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) stain, original magnification ×10.

Histopathology of an original biopsy from an eyelid shows classic lentigo maligna, also known as melanoma in situ, stained with Hematoxylin and eosin (10X magnification). Key features include an increased number of melanocytes forming nests at the dermoepidermal junction, as well as presenting as single cells with mildly enlarged, hyperchromatic nuclei.

Histopathology showing desmoplastic melanoma, characterized by atypical spindle cells embedded in a dense fibrous matrix. The image is stained with haematoxylin and eosin at an original magnification of ×10.

Histopathology showing focal cellular necrosis in a Spitz tumor, identified as a risk-associated histopathological feature and proposed as a prognostic parameter. Stained with hematoxylin and eosin, original magnification ×400.

Histopathology of a lesion from a 74-year-old male, initially submitted with a pretest diagnosis of “indeterminate; favor irritated compound nevus with moderate to severe atypia.” All three reviewing dermatopathologists classified the lesion as melanoma, with a score of +2.4. Following the test, the submitting dermatopathologist changed the diagnosis to superficial spreading melanoma. This image shows the lesion at an original magnification of x200, stained with Hematoxylin-eosin.

Histopathology showing a subepidermal lesion with subtle pigmentation. This lesion was diagnosed as melanoma arising in a blue nevus, characterized by atypical spindle cells of intermediate to low pigmentation, minimal stroma, sparse lymphocytic infiltration, and growing in a nest-like pattern with high mitotic activity. Stained with hematoxylin and eosin, original magnification 40×.

Histopathology of melanoma, superficial spreading type, showing nests of atypical melanocytic proliferation projecting from the basal layer of the epidermis. Stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at 100X magnification.

Histopathology (hematoxylin and eosin stain, x10 magnification) of the orbital mass, showing the blue nevus component. This component is characterized by interweaving cells with increased cellularity and heavily pigmented spindle cells, similar to a previous biopsy. Diagnostic pigmented spindle-shaped dendritic cells are visible at the left upper corner, adjacent to amelanotic uniform oval to spindle cells with inconspicuous nucleoli and clear cytoplasm.

High-power hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) stain showing atypical features in a compound vulvar nevus in a patient aged 14 years, in a background of juvenile vulvar lichen sclerosus (JVLS). This view allows for detailed examination of the cytological and architectural characteristics of the nevus, which often present as moderate to severe atypia in cases with VLS. Bar = 0.100 mm.

Histologic examination of lesion D shows the characteristics of in situ malignant melanoma, with marked proliferation of atypical melanocytes along the dermal-epidermal junction and superficial inflammatory infiltration of the dermis. Ulceration and satellite nodules were absent, lymphovascular or perineural invasion were not seen, and the margins were negative. (Hematoxylin & eosin stain, x100 magnification).

Histopathology of a superficial spreading melanoma (SSM) from case 2, stained with H&E at ×40 magnification. The image details epithelioid to spindle-shaped melanocytes with pale, eosinophilic cytoplasm. There is variation in the size and shape of the cells, which exhibit large nuclei with prominent nucleoli and nuclear vacuoles, indicating slight nuclear atypia. A few multinucleated cells are also visible, consistent with the detailed microscopical findings for this case.

Microphotograph of the inflamed juvenile conjunctival nevus from Case No. 6 (boy patient, 13 years old). The histological section shows compound nevoid melanocytic proliferation made up of intraepithelial and subepithelial melanocytic nests organized around solid or cystic epithelial inclusions that also contain goblet cells. There is also a dense, diffuse, stromal inflammatory infiltrate with lymphocytes, plasma cells, and eosinophils. At the base of the lesion, lymphocytes are organized into a lymphoid follicle with a germinal center. Hematoxylin–Eosin (HE) staining, original magnification ×100.

Histopathology of a Spitz tumor demonstrating epidermal ulceration, which is identified as a risk-associated histopathological feature and a proposed prognostic parameter indicating increased clinical risk for patients. Stained with hematoxylin and eosin, original magnification ×125.

Left forearm excisional biopsy findings: Hematoxylin and eosin staining showing the proliferation of spindle-shaped, non-pigmented nevomelanocytes in the papillary dermis (arrows). Magnification: ×100.
Histopathology showing lentigo maligna melanoma (LMM) with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining, viewed at x100 magnification.

Histopathology of sentinel lymph node deposits from a patient with an atypical deep penetrating nevus, stained with Hematoxylin and eosin at 200× magnification. Multiple clusters of coarsely and densely pigmented epithelioid cells are noted within the lymph node parenchyma and subcapsular space. Atypical deep penetrating nevi may show nodal deposits on sentinel lymph node biopsy.

Histopathology of a 5-µm section from formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded tissue, stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at ×100 magnification, showing a deep penetrating nevus. Nests of spindle cells and epithelioid-like cells are observed as deep dermal and subcutaneous nested proliferations, arranged in a wedge-shaped pattern. These nests diffusely penetrate and infiltrate the surrounding tissues, including the deep subcutaneous adipose tissue on the left. Note the abundance of melanophages along with some pigment incontinence.
Histopathology showing the intraepidermic component of lentigo maligna melanoma. The image is stained with Haematoxylin Eosin and viewed at 200x magnification.

medsiglip_fail_biomedclip_ok.caption: Histopathology of melanocytic hyperplasia (lentigo), showing a tangential biopsy of a lentigo of the matrix with heavily pigmented melanocytes present in the matrix epithelium. Stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E), original magnification ×4.

Histopathology showing an overview of lentigo malignant melanoma (LMM), stained with hematoxylin-eosin (x2 magnification). This image displays the proliferation of atypical melanocytes, seen singly and in nests along the basal layer of the epidermis, and epitheloid nests in the adjacent dermis. The atypical cells are small with prominent nuclei, confirming the diagnosis of LMM.
Histopathology at higher magnification (200×), stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E), specifically showing sebaceous glands, which are part of the nevus sebaceus.

Histopathological analysis showing a neoplasm consisting of basophilic cells, forming well-developed nests and masses that infiltrate the dermis, without affecting the epidermis. This is compatible with nodular melanoma of the skin. The image is stained with Hematoxylin & eosin, shown at ×40 and ×100 magnification.

High-power view histopathology from the spleen of a 72-year-old male patient, illustrating the morphology of the metastatic malignant neoplasm. It shows round, short spindly, and ovoid tumor cells arranged in vague solid and fascicular patterns, with pink and eosinophilic cytoplasm. This splenic lesion represents a rare isolated metastasis from a cutaneous malignant melanoma diagnosed 25 years prior. Hematoxylin and eosin-stained section, magnification × 40.

Histopathology (40× hematoxylin & eosin [H&E] stain) illustrating a striking acrosyringial and periductal distribution of the melanocytic neoplasm. The final histopathological diagnosis was acral compound melanocytic nevus with congenital features.

Histopathology at 40x magnification showing a proliferation of bland-appearing spindled cells within the dermis surrounded by loose collagenous stroma, which is consistent with neurofibroma (black arrows). This finding is observed in conjunction with a nevus sebaceus. The image is stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E).

Histopathology of acral lentiginous melanoma as seen in a hematoxylin and eosin-stained slide. The epidermis can be seen detaching from the dermis, giving the illusion of a blister, which is called an “unzipping” artifact and indicates confluent growth.

Acral melanoma. Histopathology showing in situ acral lentiginous melanoma (ALM), characterized by a lentiginous growth pattern. This image displays a continuous proliferation of atypical melanocytes with enlarged hyperchromatic nuclei and prominent dendrites along the dermoepidermal junction (arrowhead). At this late stage of the lesion, there is pagetoid spread and scattered junctional nests at the tips of the rete ridges (arrow), where the confluence of cells has increased. Established lesions also show acanthosis, hypergranulosis, and hyperkeratosis, with marked elongation of the rete ridges. (H&E, ×100).

Histopathologic examination of Case 1, a superficial spreading type melanoma with a MAP2K1 in-frame deletion, shows expansile nested growth along the epidermis with associated epidermal effacement. This image demonstrates epidermal involvement, including extension beyond the dermal component, confluent growth along the basal epidermis with rete effacement, and expansile intraepidermal nests. Stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E).

Histopathology image showing irregular islands of infiltrating melanoma cells. A vague pseudo glandular pattern is also visible. The section is stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at ×200 magnification.

Histopathology of a vulval excision for melanoma, specifically depicting a superficially spreading melanoma. The image shows multiple clusters of atypical melanocytes within the epidermis. The section is stained with haematoxylin and eosin at an original magnification of x40.

Histopathology of the brownish-black solid mass within the ovarian cyst reveals infiltrating nests of pleomorphic cells with prominent nucleoli and black pigments. Junctional activity is also present. These features are characteristic of malignant melanoma originating from the mature cystic teratoma of the ovary, with cytomorphologic features including prominent nucleoli. (H&E stain, original magnification × 400).

Histopathology showing abundant melanin in deep cells of a Spitz tumor, identified as a risk-associated histopathological feature and proposed as a prognostic parameter. Stained with hematoxylin and eosin, original magnification ×200.

Histopathology showing the presence of micrometastasis (microsatellitosis) of melanoma located deep to the primary tumor. Micrometastasis is defined as nests of tumor cells adjacent or deep to the primary tumor, identified as a discontinuous nest separated from the primary tumor by normal skin. Stained with hematoxylin and eosin, original magnification ×100.

biomedclip_fail_medsiglip_ok.caption: Histopathology of a 61-year-old female's back, showing late-onset lentiginous and nested melanoma. The image reveals a chaotic architecture with variably sized nests and confluent single nevoid melanocytes that extend along broad junctional zones where the rete ridges are absent, producing a lentiginous pattern. Key features include loss of rete ridges and confluent melanocytes. This is an H&E stained digital scan at ×2 magnification.

Hematoxylin-eosin staining of the right buccal mucosa from the proband’s father, revealing histological features consistent with White Spongy Nevus (WSN). Panel (d) shows spinous cells with nuclear pyknosis (top arrow) and cell edema (bottom arrow). These findings, including nuclei consolidation and edematous spiny cells, are characteristic pathological manifestations of WSN. Original magnification: 400×.

Photomicrograph of a Spitz nevus harboring ALK fusion, showing a dermal melanocytic proliferation composed of nonpigmented, large, epithelioid and spindle melanocytes with amphophilic cytoplasm, vesicular nuclei, and prominent nucleoli, arranged in plexiform intersecting fascicles. The presence of non-pigmented, large, spindle melanocytes with pericellular clefts, amphophilic cytoplasm, vesicular nuclei, and prominent nucleoli, growing in plexiform intersecting fascicles, appears as a hallmark of ALK fusions. The epidermis is often hyperplastic and pagetoid spread is usually absent. Nuclear pleomorphism is usually mild and rarely moderate. H&E staining; original magnification 100×.
Histopathology revealing melanocyte nests within the matrix epithelium, consistent with melanocytic nevi. The image is stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) and captured at ×40 magnification.

Histopathology showing cerebroid nests of melanocytes (CNMs) in nodular melanoma, observed during a morphological comparison between poorly differentiated clusters (PDCs) and CNMs. CNMs are readily detectable and display a morphology similar to PDCs in colorectal carcinoma. These CNMs, an active growth front of nodular melanoma, are characterized by atypical pleomorphic melanocytic cells with large cytoplasm, spindle or epithelioid shape, and prominent nucleoli, often detaching from the main mass with an infiltrative appearance. CNMs are associated with lymphatic vascular invasion, higher Clark level (Clark’s IV and V), epithelioid phenotype, aggressive growth, and clinically characterized by recurrence and metastasis. The image is a Hematoxylin and eosin stained slide at 20x magnification, with a scale bar of 100 µm.

Hematoxylin-eosin staining of the right buccal mucosa from the proband’s father, revealing histological features consistent with White Spongy Nevus (WSN). Panel (c) highlights vacuolar change of spinous cells, indicated by the red arrow. The presence of enlarged and vacuolated spiny cells is a characteristic pathological manifestation of WSN. Original magnification: 200×.

Skin biopsy (10× magnification) stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin revealing epidermal malignant epithelial neoplasia. Tumor cells, which are large with abundant clear/pale cytoplasm, pleomorphic nuclei, and prominent nucleoli, are observed. These cells are arranged in small nests and isolated forms across all levels of the epidermis, exhibiting pagetoid migration. Histological sections also revealed dermal invasion by individual cells and glandular structures.

Histopathology of a central lesion from a 15-year-old female patient with a pigmented lesion on the lateral aspect of the left arm. The image shows spindle-shaped and dendritic, strongly pigmented melanocytes amid dense fibrous stroma and melanophages, predominantly occupying the upper and mid dermis. These features contribute to the diagnosis of common blue nevus with satellitosis. Stained with Hematoxylin & eosin, original magnification X40.
Histopathology of the main lesion showing periappendageal and perivascular concentrations of nevus cells. Hematoxylin and eosin (HE) stain. Original magnification ×100.

Histopathology (hematoxylin and eosin stain, 20× magnification) of a nevus-associated melanoma from a 76-year-old woman. The image shows an asymmetric melanocytic lesion composed on the left side by a dermal proliferation of monomorphous melanocytes with maturation, which corresponds to the central hypopigmented-brown structureless areas observed dermoscopically. On the right shoulder, elongated rete-ridges are present with atypical melanocytes in single units and irregular nests throughout all epidermal layers, correlating to the atypical network seen in the dermoscopic image.

Histopathology of a dermally located pigmented lesion, identified as a sclerotic blue nevus, from a 48-year-old female's 8mm bluish lesion on the left leg. The lesion was initially suspected to be a fibrous histiocytoma but showed morphological characteristics similar to a dendritic blue nevus with pronounced sclerotic stroma upon pathological examination. Stained with hematoxylin and eosin, original magnification 40×.

Detailed view of the remaining intradermal nevus and the fibrosis, which runs parallel to the direction of the epidermis. This image provides a higher magnification of features seen in the traumatized nevus from a 44-year-old female patient's thigh. The presence of fibrosis is consistent with a traumatized melanocytic lesion, highlighting the need for comprehensive clinical correlation, including patient age, lesion location, and any history of trauma, which can influence microscopic interpretation. The image is stained with Hematoxylin & eosin at ×200 magnification.

Microscopic image of histopathology for Case 21, an 82-year-old male presenting with lentigo maligna melanoma (LMM) on the forehead. The image, stained with H&E and viewed at 100x magnification, shows the tumor at Level 1 with a Breslow thickness of 0.5 mm.
Histopathological findings showing on the base a focus with nests of a conventional nevus. Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) stain, x 20 magnification.

Histopathologic findings of cutaneous melanoma from an individual with a germline variant in POT1 (p.His226Asn). Low magnification shows spitzoid melanoma with epidermal and dermal nests, growing adjacent to a nevus, marked by the arrows. Stained with hematoxylin and eosin, original magnification: X4.

Histopathology showing the presence of micrometastasis (microsatellitosis) of melanoma located deep to the primary tumor. Micrometastasis is defined as nests of tumor cells adjacent or deep to the primary tumor, identified as a discontinuous nest separated from the primary tumor by normal skin. Stained with hematoxylin and eosin, original magnification ×200.

Histopathology of the cheek lesion, diagnosed as a cellular blue nevus. Panel C shows spindle cell melanocytes with subtle pigment deposition. These tumor cells are oval to spindle-shaped, featuring small monomorphic nuclei, speckled chromatin, and light or finely pigmented cytoplasm. A distinct rim of sclerotic stroma surrounds the nests of these cells, which are also accompanied by pigmented macrophages. Staining is hematoxylin and eosin, original magnification 200×.

Microscopic image of histopathology for Case 21, an 82-year-old male presenting with lentigo maligna melanoma (LMM) on the forehead. The image, stained with H&E and viewed at 100x magnification, shows the tumor at Level 4 with a Breslow thickness of 0.9 mm. This increased Breslow thickness (compared to 0.5 mm on Level 1 in panel a) resulted in the upstaging of the patient from pT1a to pT1b.

Histopathology of nodular melanoma showing Cerebroid nests of melanocytes (CNMs) graded as G1, characterized by 0 to 4 buds. The image is a hematoxylin and eosin stained slide at 20x magnification with a 100 µm scale bar.
Histopathology showing nests of nevus cells with overlying normal epidermis. The nevus cells have a large nucleus and scanty eosinophilic cytoplasm. (Hematoxylin and Eosin stain, 10x magnification).

Histopathology of a superficial spreading melanoma (T1a, Breslow 0.53 mm) from the left chest of a 44-year-old man. Superficial spreading melanoma is characterized by an initial radial growth phase (RGP) with atypical melanocytes. This hematoxylin and eosin (HE) stained image (100× magnification) shows peripheral, confluent, and heavily pigmented atypical junctional nests of melanocytes. These nests correlate with pseudopods observed in dermoscopy. Additionally, structureless black and blue areas seen in dermoscopy are typically related to a heavy band-like lymphohistiocytic infiltrate with melanophages in the dermis.
